title: Software Bill of Materials Signals — Container Image Digests

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST CSF ID.AM-02 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST CSF ID.AM-02 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.7.17: Software Bill of Materials Signals — Container Image Digests.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.7.17' running on kube:* sourcetype, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=containers sourcetype=kube:* earliest=-6h
| stats latest(image_id) as image by pod, namespace
| lookup approved_container_images.csv image OUTPUT tier
| where isnull(tier)
description: Correlates Kubernetes workload image digests to approved golden images for containerized asset inventory depth.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST CSF compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes containers and sourcetypes kube:*. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Export digest list from artifact registry; (2) alert on unknown digests; (3) feed asset register; (4) map to business owner namespace labels. Indexes required: `index=containers`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=kube:*`; Lookups: `approved_container_images`, `approved_container_images.csv`.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for correlates kubernetes workload image digests to approved golden images for containerized asset inventory depth. This helps maintain steady compliance posture and reduces manual review effort.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: OTel k8s attributes or `kube:audit`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `kube:*` sourcetype.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Export digest list from artifact registry; (2) alert on unknown digests; (3) feed asset register; (4) map to business owner namespace labels.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=containers sourcetype=kube:* earliest=-6h
| stats latest(image_id) as image by pod, namespace
| lookup approved_container_images.csv image OUTPUT tier
| where isnull(tier)
```

Understanding this SPL

**Software Bill of Materials Signals — Container Image Digests (ID.AM-02)** — Correlates Kubernetes workload image digests to approved golden images for containerized asset inventory depth.

Documented **Data sources**: `kube:*` sourcetype. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): OTel k8s attributes or `kube:audit`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: containers; **sourcetype**: kube:*. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=containers, sourcetype=kube:*, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by pod, namespace** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(tier)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Heatmap
